items.explanation:
31st President (1929–1933), Republican
Trained as a mining engineer — one of the few presidents with a technical background
Led massive humanitarian relief efforts feeding millions in Europe after World War I before entering politics
Presided over the 1929 stock market crash and the onset of the Great Depression
Shantytowns of the unemployed became known as "Hoovervilles" as a term of blame
Lived 31 years after leaving office — the longest post-presidency until Jimmy Carter surpassed him
Spoke Mandarin Chinese with his wife Lou to keep conversations private in the White House
Deeply unpopular when he left office, but later rehabilitated his reputation through public service

32nd President (1933–1945), Democrat
Only president elected four times — his unprecedented tenure led to the 22nd Amendment limiting presidents to two terms
Launched the New Deal, a sweeping set of programs to combat the Great Depression (Social Security, WPA, SEC, FDIC)
Led the United States through most of World War II, dying in office on April 12, 1945, just weeks before V-E Day
Paralyzed from the waist down by polio (diagnosed 1921) — used a wheelchair but was rarely photographed in it
Pioneered "fireside chats," radio addresses that made him a reassuring presence in American homes
Married to Eleanor Roosevelt, his fifth cousin once removed, who became the most activist First Lady in history
Face on the dime

33rd President (1945–1953), Democrat
Ordered the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki (August 1945), ending World War II
Desegregated the U.S. military by executive order in 1948
Led the U.S. into the Korean War (1950–1953)
Kept a sign on his desk reading "The Buck Stops Here"
Left office in 1953 with a 22% approval rating — the lowest of any president at that time — though historians now rank him highly
Never attended college — the last president without a college degree
Was a haberdasher (men's clothing store owner) before entering politics
Nicknamed "Give 'em Hell Harry" for his combative campaign style in the 1948 upset victory over Dewey

34th President (1953–1961), Republican
Supreme Allied Commander in World War II who planned and oversaw the D-Day invasion of Normandy (June 6, 1944)
Created the Interstate Highway System (1956), the largest public works project in American history
His farewell address warned of the dangers of the "military-industrial complex"
Sent federal troops to Little Rock, Arkansas to enforce school desegregation in 1957
Campaign slogan "I Like Ike" became one of the most iconic in presidential history
Both the Democratic and Republican parties tried to recruit him as their nominee before he chose the GOP
NASA was created during his presidency (1958) in response to the Soviet launch of Sputnik

35th President (1961–1963), Democrat
Youngest person ever elected president at age 43 (Theodore Roosevelt was younger when he assumed office, but was not elected to his first term)
First Catholic president
Navigated the Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962), the closest the world came to nuclear war
Created the Peace Corps by executive order in 1961
Assassinated in Dallas, Texas on November 22, 1963 — a date seared into American memory
"Ask not what your country can do for you — ask what you can do for your country" from his inaugural address
WWII hero who saved his crew after PT-109 was sunk by a Japanese destroyer
Won the Pulitzer Prize for "Profiles in Courage" (1957), though the extent of his authorship is debated

36th President (1963–1969), Democrat
Signed the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 — landmark legislation ending legal segregation
His "Great Society" programs created Medicare, Medicaid, and the War on Poverty
Escalation of the Vietnam War destroyed his presidency and divided the nation
Assumed office after JFK's assassination — sworn in aboard Air Force One with Jackie Kennedy standing beside him
Won the 1964 election by the largest popular vote margin in modern history
Known for a crude, dominating personality — the "Johnson Treatment" was his method of physically cornering people to get their votes
Chose not to run for reelection in 1968, announcing his withdrawal in a televised address

37th President (1969–1974), Republican
Only president to resign from office, leaving in disgrace over the Watergate scandal (August 9, 1974)
Opened diplomatic relations with the People's Republic of China in 1972 — a historic foreign policy shift
Created the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) in 1970
Won reelection in 1972 carrying 49 out of 50 states — one of the largest landslides in history
Infamously declared "I am not a crook" during a press conference
Pardoned by Gerald Ford, sparing him from criminal prosecution
Served as Eisenhower's Vice President (1953–1961) and lost to JFK in 1960 in the first televised presidential debate

38th President (1974–1977), Republican
Only president never elected as either president or vice president — appointed VP after Spiro Agnew resigned, then became president when Nixon resigned
Pardoned Richard Nixon for all crimes related to Watergate — a decision that likely cost him the 1976 election
Survived two assassination attempts within 17 days in September 1975 (by Lynette "Squeaky" Fromme and Sara Jane Moore)
Was a star football player at the University of Michigan — turned down offers from the NFL's Detroit Lions and Green Bay Packers
Born Leslie Lynch King Jr. — his name was changed after his mother remarried Gerald Rudolff Ford Sr.
The only president from Michigan

39th President (1977–1981), Democrat
Brokered the Camp David Accords (1978), achieving a historic peace treaty between Israel and Egypt
Presidency overshadowed by the Iran hostage crisis (1979–1981), in which 52 Americans were held for 444 days
Won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2002 for decades of humanitarian work through the Carter Center
Was a peanut farmer from Plains, Georgia, and a trained nuclear submarine officer in the Navy
Installed solar panels on the White House roof — Ronald Reagan later had them removed
Longest-lived president in American history — died on December 29, 2024 at age 100
First president from the Deep South since Zachary Taylor (1849)

40th President (1981–1989), Republican
"The Great Communicator" — a former Hollywood actor and president of the Screen Actors Guild before entering politics
Oldest president at inauguration (69) until Donald Trump (70) and then Joe Biden (78) surpassed him
Survived an assassination attempt on March 30, 1981, quipping to surgeons "I hope you're all Republicans"
Challenged Gorbachev to "tear down this wall" at the Berlin Wall in 1987
Iran-Contra scandal: his administration secretly sold arms to Iran and funneled profits to Nicaraguan rebels
Enacted sweeping tax cuts and a massive military buildup during the Cold War
Won 49 states in his 1984 reelection, losing only Minnesota and D.C.
Diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease in 1994 after leaving office; was a Democrat until switching to Republican in 1962

41st President (1989–1993), Republican
Last World War II veteran to serve as president — was a Navy torpedo bomber pilot shot down over the Pacific
Former CIA Director (1976–1977) before becoming Reagan's Vice President
Famous broken promise: "Read my lips: no new taxes" — then raised taxes, which contributed to his 1992 reelection loss
Led the U.S. through the Gulf War (Operation Desert Storm, 1991), liberating Kuwait from Iraq
Father of George W. Bush (#43) — the second father-son presidential pair after John and John Quincy Adams
Parachuted from an airplane on his 90th birthday as part of a series of celebratory skydives
Overseen the end of the Cold War and the fall of the Berlin Wall during his presidency

42nd President (1993–2001), Democrat
Second president to be impeached (after Andrew Johnson) — acquitted by the Senate on charges related to the Monica Lewinsky scandal
Presided over the longest peacetime economic expansion in American history
Balanced the federal budget and achieved a surplus — the first since 1969
Signed NAFTA (North American Free Trade Agreement) into law
Campaign strategist James Carville's mantra "It's the economy, stupid" defined his 1992 victory
Rhodes Scholar who studied at Oxford University
Played saxophone on The Arsenio Hall Show during the 1992 campaign — a defining moment of pop-culture politics
First Baby Boomer president (born 1946)

43rd President (2001–2009), Republican
Won the disputed 2000 election decided by Bush v. Gore — the Supreme Court halted a Florida recount, giving him the presidency by 537 votes
Led the post-9/11 response, launching wars in Afghanistan (2001) and Iraq (2003)
Signed No Child Left Behind (2002) and the TARP bailout during the 2008 financial crisis
Former governor of Texas
Son of George H.W. Bush (#41) — only the second son of a president to become president (after John Quincy Adams)
Also the second president to win the Electoral College while losing the popular vote in the modern era (after Benjamin Harrison in 1888)
Before entering politics, co-owned the Texas Rangers baseball team

44th President (2009–2017), Democrat
First African American president in U.S. history
Signed the Affordable Care Act (2010), commonly known as "Obamacare," the most significant healthcare reform since Medicare
Ordered the military operation that killed Osama bin Laden in Abbottabad, Pakistan (May 2, 2011)
Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2009, early in his first term
Former U.S. Senator from Illinois — served only one partial Senate term before winning the presidency
Campaign slogan "Yes We Can" became a cultural phenomenon
Born in Honolulu, Hawaii — the first president born outside the contiguous United States
Negotiated the Paris Climate Agreement (2015) and the Iran nuclear deal (JCPOA)

45th and 47th President — first president to serve non-consecutive terms since Grover Cleveland (#22/#24)
First president with no prior military or government service before taking office
First president impeached twice (2019 and 2021) — acquitted by the Senate both times
Won the 2016 election as a political outsider, defeating Hillary Clinton despite losing the popular vote
Built his career as a real estate developer and television personality (hosted "The Apprentice")
Presidency shaped by the COVID-19 pandemic beginning in 2020
Lost the 2020 election to Joe Biden; the January 6, 2021 Capitol breach followed the certification of electoral votes
Signed the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (2017), the largest tax overhaul since 1986

46th President (2021–2025), Democrat
Oldest person ever inaugurated as president at age 78
Served as Vice President under Barack Obama for two full terms (2009–2017)
First ran for president in 1988 — took over three decades to finally win the office
Served as a U.S. Senator from Delaware for 36 years (1973–2009), one of the longest Senate tenures in history
Oversaw the COVID-19 vaccine rollout and signed the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law
Withdrew from the 2024 presidential race, endorsing Vice President Kamala Harris
Lost his first wife Neilia and infant daughter Naomi in a 1972 car accident, and his son Beau died of brain cancer in 2015

Same person as #45 — second president to serve non-consecutive terms (after Grover Cleveland, #22 and #24)
Won the 2024 presidential election, defeating Kamala Harris
Oldest person to win a presidential election at age 78, surpassing Joe Biden's record
First president to return to office after being voted out since Grover Cleveland won in 1892
